en.extract:
The large gray babbler (Argya malcolmi) is a member of the family Leiothrichidae found across India and far western Nepal. They are locally common in the scrub, open forest and gardenland. They are usually seen in small groups and are easily distinguished from other babblers in the region by their nasal call and the whitish outer feathers to their long tail. It is one of the largest babblers in the region.


Description
This long-tailed and large babbler has a brown body with creamy white outer tail feathers which are easily visible as they fly with fluttery wing beats low over the ground. The lores are dark and forehead is grey with white shaft streaks on the feathers. The rump and uppertail covers are pale grey. The mantle has dusky blotches and no shaft streaks. The three outer tail feathers are white and the fourth pair has the outer web white. The wings are darker brown. The iris is yellow and the upper mandible is dark brown while the lower mandible is yellowish. The tail is faintly cross barred. Abnormal specimens showing albinism or leucism have been reported.
The species was first described from the Deccan Plateau region. The scientific name was given by Colonel W H Sykes in appreciation of support that he received from Major-General Sir John Malcolm.


Distribution and habitat
Found throughout the Indian subcontinent, south of the Himalayas, east of the Thar desert until Bihar. The species is not found in Kerala and records from the Sind area are not confirmed. It is found mainly in open dry scrub forest and cultivated areas. It is not found in the eastern dry zone of Tamil Nadu; however, an exhausted specimen has been observed in Pondicherry.


Behaviour and ecology

The species is found in small flocks which keep in contact with loud nasal calls. Members of the flock may join in defending against predators. Individuals may also attack their own reflections. They are mostly seen in open scrub country where they forage on or close to the ground. They hop and leap on the ground in search of prey. Individuals in a group may indulge in play behaviour. They feed mainly on insects but also feed on small lizards, molluscs and arachnids. They also feed on seeds, grains and berries. They are found in gardens within some cities such as Pune and Ahmedabad. In other cities such as Bangalore, they are seen only on the outskirts of the expanding urban zone.
They are known to breed through the year but mainly during the rainy season from March to September. The usual clutch is four eggs. The nest is a shallow cup placed in a shrub often of thorny species. Their nests are parasitized by the pied cuckoo and the common hawk-cuckoo. It has been suggested that more than one female may lay eggs in a single nest and help in incubation; however, this has not been verified.
Although secure with a wide distribution, some local populations are threatened by hunting for meat. An endoparasitic cestode Vogea vestibularis has been described from this species.


References


Other sources
Gupta, R. C. Midha, M. (1997) Breeding Biology of Large Grey Babbler, Turdoides malcolmi. Geobios (Jodhpur, India) 24(4):214-218.
Gupta, RC; Midha, Meenu (1995) Drinking and bathing behaviour of Large Grey Babbler Turdoides malcolmi (Sykes). Zoos' Print Journal 10(5):23.
Gupta, RC; Midha, Meenu (1994) Observations on the behaviour of Large Grey Babbler, Turdoides malcolmi (Sykes). Cheetal 33(2):42-51.


External links

Photos and videos

The '''large gray babbler''' ('''''Argya malcolmi''''') is a member of the family [[Leiothrichidae]] found across India and far western Nepal. They are locally common in the scrub, open forest and gardenland. They are usually seen in small groups and are easily distinguished from other babblers in the region by their nasal call and the whitish outer feathers to their long tail. It is one of the largest babblers in the region.<ref name=pcr/>

==Description==
This long-tailed and large babbler has a brown body with creamy white outer tail feathers which are easily visible as they fly with fluttery wing beats low over the ground. The lores are dark and forehead is grey with white shaft streaks on the feathers. The rump and uppertail covers are pale grey. The mantle has dusky blotches and no shaft streaks.<ref name=pcr>{{Cite book|author1=Rasmussen, P.C.  |author2=Anderton J. C. |year=2005 |title= Birds of South Asia. The Ripley Guide. Vol. 2|page=444|publisher= Smithsonian Institution & Lynx Edicions}}</ref> The three outer tail feathers are white and the fourth pair has the outer web white. The wings are darker brown. The iris is yellow and the upper mandible is dark brown while the lower mandible is yellowish. The tail is faintly cross barred.<ref name=hbk/> Abnormal specimens showing [[albinism]] or [[leucism]] have been reported.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=Total albinism in large grey babbler ''Turdoides malcolmi''|author=Sharma, S. K. |journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. |year=2003 | volume =100|issue=1| pages=144–145|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48602718}}</ref>

The species was first described from the [[Deccan Plateau]] region. The scientific name was given by [[William Henry Sykes|Colonel W H Sykes]] in appreciation of support that he received from Major-General Sir [[John Malcolm]].<ref>{{Cite journal|url=https://archive.org/stream/proceedingsofzoo01zool#page/88/mode/2up|page=88|journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London. Part 1|year=1830–1831|publisher=ZSL|author=Sykes, W.H.|title=Catalogue of birds of the raptorial and insessorial orders (systematically arranged,) observed in the Dukhun by Lt. Col. W.H. Sykes}}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
Found throughout the [[Indian subcontinent]], south of the [[Himalayas]], east of the Thar desert until [[Bihar]]. The species is not found in Kerala and records from the Sind area are not confirmed. It is found mainly in open dry scrub forest and cultivated areas.<ref name=hbk>{{Cite book|author1=Ali, S  |author2=S D Ripley| title=Handbook of the birds of India and Pakistan|edition=2nd|publisher=Oxford University Press|volume= 6|year= 1996 |pages =220–222 }}</ref> It is not found in the eastern dry zone of [[Tamil Nadu]]; however, an exhausted specimen has been observed in [[Puducherry (city)|Pondicherry]].<ref>{{Cite journal| url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW16#page/n83/mode/1up/| author=Neville, Shantha| title=News from Auroville|journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] | volume=16|pages=15–16|issue=5|year=1974}}</ref>

==Behaviour and ecology==
[[File:Large Grey Babbler in Saswad August 2025 by Tisha Mukherjee 02.jpg|thumb|left| Large Grey Babbler (''Argya malcolmi'') eating insect in [[Saswad]], Maharashtra, India.]]
[[File:Large Grey Babbler (Turdoides malcolmi)- allopreening in AP W IMG 8050.jpg|thumb|Allopreening ([[India]])]]

The species is found in small flocks which keep in contact with loud nasal calls. Members of the flock may join in defending against predators.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Dharmakumarsinhji, RS|author-link=Raol Shri Dharmakumarsinhji|year=1961 |title= Communal distraction display in Large Grey Babbler, [''Turdoides malcolmi'' (Sykes)]|journal= J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. |volume=58|issue=2|page=512|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/47541100}}</ref> Individuals may also attack their own reflections.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Alexander, HG |author-link=Horace Gundry Alexander|year=1950|title= Large Grey Babbler attacking metal hub-cap of wheel of car |journal= J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. |volume=49|issue=3|page=550|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48182064}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ali, Hamid A |year=1951 |title= Large Grey Babbler attacking metal hub-cap of car|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=50|issue=1|page=171|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48056674}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|author=Singh, JL |year=1977 |title= Grey Babblers pecking at window panes|url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW38_6#page/n13/mode/1up |journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] |volume=17|issue=10|page=12}}</ref> They are mostly seen in open scrub country where they forage on or close to the ground. They hop and leap on the ground in search of prey.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Bharos, AMK |year=1996|title= Sideways leap-frogging by the Large Grey Babblers, ''Turdoides malcolmi'' (Sykes)|journal= J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. |volume=93|issue=1|page=93|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48603341}}</ref> Individuals in a group may indulge in [[play behaviour]].<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Gaston, A. J. |year=1977|title=Social behaviour within groups of jungle babblers ''Turdoides striatus''|journal= Animal Behaviour|volume=25|pages=828–848|doi=10.1016/0003-3472(77)90036-7|s2cid=53146659}}</ref> They feed mainly on insects but also feed on small lizards, molluscs and arachnids. They also feed on seeds, grains and berries.<ref>{{Cite journal|doi=10.1007/BF03179379|author1=Toor, HS |author2=Saini, MS |year=1986 |title= Feeding ecology of the Large Grey Babbler ''Turdoides malcolmi''|journal= Proc. Indian Acad. Sci. Anim. Sci. |volume=95|issue=4|pages=429–436 |s2cid=84465452 |url=http://www.ias.ac.in/jarch/procb/95a/429-436.pdf}}</ref> They are found in gardens within some cities such as [[Pune]] and [[Ahmedabad]]. In other cities such as [[Bangalore]], they are seen only on the outskirts of the expanding urban zone.

They are known to breed through the year but mainly during the rainy season from March to September.<ref name=pcr/> The usual clutch is four eggs. The nest is a shallow cup placed in a shrub often of thorny species. Their nests are [[brood parasitism|parasitized]] by the [[Jacobin cuckoo|pied cuckoo]]<ref>{{Cite book|author=Whistler, Hugh |year=1949|url=https://archive.org/stream/popularhandbooko033226mbp#page/n81/mode/2up |pages=45–46 |publisher=Gurney and Jackson|title=Popular handbook of Indian birds|isbn=1-4067-4576-6}}</ref> and the [[common hawk-cuckoo]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://archive.org/stream/birdsindia03oaterich#page/214/mode/2up|title= The Fauna of British India, Including Ceylon and Burma. Birds Volume 3. |year=1895|pages=213–214|publisher=Taylor and Francis, London|author=Blanford, WT}}</ref> It has been suggested that more than one female may lay eggs in a single nest and help in incubation; however, this has not been verified.<ref name=hbk/><ref>{{Cite journal|author=Macdonald, Malcolm |year=1959| title= Communal nest-feeding in Babblers| journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume =56| issue=1|pages=132–134|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/47374994}}</ref><ref name=fbi2>{{Cite book|author=Baker ECS|title= The Fauna of British India, Including Ceylon and Burma. Birds. Volume 1|edition=2nd|pages=200–201|year=1922|url=https://archive.org/stream/BakerFbiBirds1/bakerFBI1#page/n230/mode/1up}}</ref>

Although secure with a wide distribution, some local populations are threatened by hunting for meat.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=Large grey babbler (''Turdoides malcolmi'') trapped for the table|author=Bharos, A. M. K.|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|year=2001| volume=98 | issue=3 |page=452|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48583677}}</ref> An endoparasitic [[cestode]] ''Vogea vestibularis'' has been described from this species.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=''Vogea vestibularis'' n. g., n. sp., a Dilepidid Cestode from the Intestine of the Large Grey Babbler, ''Argya malcolmi''|jstor=3274500|author=Johri, GN|journal=The Journal of Parasitology |volume=45|issue=3 |year=1959|pages=287–290|doi=10.2307/3274500|pmid=13665467}}</ref>

zh.extract:
灰鸫鹛（学名：Turdoides malcolmi），是画眉科鸫鹛属的一种，分布于印度、尼泊尔和巴基斯坦。该物种的保护状况被评为无危。
灰鸫鹛的平均体重约为69.3克。栖息地包括种植园、干燥的稀树草原、乡村花园、耕地和亚热带或热带的（低地）干燥疏灌丛。


参考文献

Turdoides malcolmi. BirdLife.org.   [2011-06-27]. （原始内容存档于2016-03-05）.
